response:
# Diagnosis or brief
Mode: create. Domain: IS. Model supplied: yes. A digital service chooses one access-friction level for all prospective users before privately informed benign and malicious users decide whether to participate.

# Proposed narrative
The service commits to a common access-friction level using the type distribution and modeled payoff functions, without observing any user's type. Friction changes participation incentives differently across latent types. More friction may deter malicious participation but can also discourage benign participation, so the service selects the level that maximizes benign-participation payoff net of malicious harm rather than assuming friction always improves safety.

# Decision structure (DFC-12)
Actor: digital-service policy owner. Trigger: committing to an access rule before users participate. Choices: one common friction level. Decision-time information: type distribution and modeled payoff functions; individual type and later participation are unknown. Stakes: platform payoff from benign participation net of malicious harm. Frictions: the same policy applies to all users. Mechanism: users strategically compare private payoffs and choose participation after the rule. Trade-off: malicious deterrence versus lost benign participation. Counterfactual: alternative common friction levels. Model mapping summary: friction is decision; private type is latent state; participation is user action/outcome; platform payoff is objective. Evidence status summary: heterogeneous friction sensitivity is a model assumption. Boundaries summary: no ex ante identification of malicious users or universal safety benefit.

# Consistency gates
Actor—pass: the service controls the common friction. Timing—pass: policy precedes user participation. Information—pass: individual types remain private. Behavior—pass: users respond strategically through modeled payoffs. Constraints—pass: one policy applies to all users. Objective—pass: the stated goal matches platform payoff. Eligible: true.

# Model mapping
common access-friction level | decision | access burden applied to every user | chosen before participation | cannot depend on private type
private user type | random_variable | benign or malicious type | latent to platform | not an observed label
participation decision | outcome | user enters or stays out | chosen after friction | strategic response, not platform choice
modeled payoff functions | parameter | type-specific participation incentives | supplied at policy choice | assumed representation
platform payoff | objective | benign-participation value net of malicious harm | maximized in expectation | not a general safety metric

# Claims
Types differ in friction sensitivity — model_assumption; source: null; retain as an assumption and test empirically before application. The service identifies each malicious user before access — unsupported_claim; source: null; remove. All friction improves safety — unsupported_claim; source: null; remove because the model permits participation losses and does not establish safety.

# Boundaries
The framing excludes direct type observation, personalized type-contingent friction, and unmodeled user responses. It does not equate platform payoff with social welfare or verified safety.

# Most important remaining weakness
The strategic payoff functions and type distribution require empirical grounding.

# Mode result
Business problem: What common access-friction level best balances benign participation against malicious participation when users have private types and respond strategically?
